risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "To match the two groups, randomization was stratified according to the body regions of the primary surgery (Table I). Subsequently, randomization was performed when the patient was in the anesthetic room immediately before surgery by using the sealed envelope method." Comment: The study explicitly describes stratified randomization and use of the sealed envelope method in a prospective randomized trial. This provides sufficient context to judge that a random allocation sequence was intended and used, meeting Low risk under a permissive standard.

_evidence.method_quote: To match the two groups, randomization was stratified according to the body regions of the primary surgery (Table I). Subsequently, randomization was performed when the patient was in the anesthetic room immediately before surgery by using the sealed envelope method.
_evidence.method_summary: Randomization was stratified by body region of primary surgery and assigned using sealed envelopes immediately before surgery.
context_clues: randomization was stratified according to the body regions of the primary surgery
randomized 57 to the endoscopy procedure group (group A) and 49 to the traditional surgery group (group B)

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "randomization was performed when the patient was in the anesthetic room immediately before surgery by using the sealed envelope method." Comment: The paper mentions a sealed envelope method, but it does not explicitly state that the envelopes were sequentially numbered and opaque; under Cochrane criteria, envelopes without all required features do not confirm adequate allocation concealment. Because concealment from the enrolling recruiter cannot be verified from the reported description, the risk is unclear.

_evidence.concealment_quote: randomization was performed when the patient was in the anesthetic room immediately before surgery by using the sealed envelope method. The surgical team in theatre were aware of group allocation, but only following the induction of anesthesia. Information on group allocation was not recorded in the clinical or surgical notes, and clinicians undertaking the follow-up of postoperative wounds were fully blinded to the group assignments.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The surgical team in theatre were aware of group allocation, but only following the induction of anesthesia. Information on group allocation was not recorded in the clinical or surgical notes, and clinicians undertaking the follow-up of postoperative wounds were fully blinded to the group assignments." Comment: Personnel delivering the intervention (the surgical team) were aware of allocation during the procedure, so blinding of personnel was incomplete. Because the study reported a subjective patient-reported outcome (VAS 7 days post-surgery), knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence at least one outcome, making performance bias high across the study overall.

_evidence.blinding_quote: The surgical team in theatre were aware of group allocation, but only following the induction of anesthesia. Information on group allocation was not recorded in the clinical or surgical notes, and clinicians undertaking the follow-up of postoperative wounds were fully blinded to the group assignments.
_evidence.blinding_method: Information on group allocation was not recorded in the clinical or surgical notes; clinicians undertaking the follow-up of postoperative wounds were fully blinded to the group assignments.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: “clinicians undertaking the follow-up of postoperative wounds were fully blinded to the group assignments.” Comment: Outcome assessment for wound follow-up was blinded, but the study also reports VAS 7 days post-surgery, a patient-reported pain outcome. Because the patient is the assessor for self-reported pain, and participants were not stated to be blinded, knowledge of allocation could influence that measurement. Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of this subjective self-reported outcome makes the overall detection bias judgement High risk despite blinding for the wound follow-up outcome.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: “clinicians undertaking the follow-up of postoperative wounds were fully blinded to the group assignments.”

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "No patients were lost during the follow-up period." Comment: The study reports complete follow-up for the randomized sample, with 57/57 analysed in group A and 49/49 analysed in group B (0% loss overall, no arm imbalance). This indicates no post-randomization attrition on the outcomes reported, so attrition is unlikely to bias the results.

per_arm_flow.reasons: No patients were lost during the follow-up period.
No patients were lost during the follow-up period.

_evidence.notes: All enrolled patients were followed up for at least 4 weeks; outcomes reported on the randomized sample with no attrition.
